title: Dude, You're Gonna Be a Dad
author: John Pfeiffer

chapters.title: The best way to ready yourself for fatherhood is to get as much information as you can about the next 9 months
chapters.text:
By the end of the second trimester, you and your spouse would have gotten your home ready for your baby's arrival. You both would probably have agreed on a name for him or her, too. Your next task is to completely learn the birthing process.

You need to have a basic knowledge of what birthing a child involves. Birthing classes and videos would be of help here. Also, take time out to tour the hospital where your spouse will be giving birth. Intricately understand the layout of the hospital and the functional areas you will be frequenting. Where's the reception? The nursery? How do the reception and nursery link? It would be helpful if you can be up-to-speed with your knowledge of first aid. You never can tell when you might need the skill. You and your partner should also ponder on all possible delivery scenarios. Does she want a vaginal birth? Or a cesarean one? What happens to the umbilical cord blood and the placenta? Agree on what to do with these by–products of the birth process and make concrete arrangements based on your agreement.

While all of the preparations are ongoing, you and your spouse should take time out to enjoy your last days together as a couple without a child. Birthing a child is a life transforming experience, for both the man and woman, as your priorities will definitely change. It won't be a bad idea to take a final holiday with your spouse, prior to her giving birth. This is also the best time to arrange some time off from work, in the period just after the birth of your child, so you can be at home to tend to your spouse or child should any of them fall ill.

Ensure your bond is strong before bringing a child in, else it will be very difficult to keep the relationship alive.

How about your finances? Give it some planning. Understand what the costs are going to be like in the months ahead, and plan accordingly. Prioritize your expenses; do away with the luxury. Start saving up, for instance, for your kid's education.

You do a lot of growing up when you’re pregnant. It’s suddenly like, ‘Yikes. Here it is, folks. Playtime is over.’ ~ Connie Fioretto

chapters.title: The female body changes during pregnancy and childbirth
chapters.text:
Understand that your spouse's body will go through a lot of physical changes during the pregnancy. These changes will be stressful and emotionally overwhelming for your spouse. Support her by loving her changing body. You do this through words of affirmation and actions that show you aren't irritated by the changes in her body. Some men make the mistake of complaining about their spouse's body during this critical time. Some even go as far as refusing to make love to their pregnant wives. It may seem like nothing to you, but these actions will hurt your spouse.

Most women are extremely body sensitive when pregnant, be careful to not say things about their bodies that will affect their self–esteem.

Don't just stop at loving her body, help her to love her body, too. Show her affection on a regular basis and reassure her whenever she complains about her body. There are other things you can do. If morning sickness is a constant bother for her, especially the type that persists deep into the day, giving her tea spiced with ginger and lemon will help alleviate her discomfort. Getting your spouse to exercise on a regular basis will also help. This is more effective if you go through the exercise routines together. Both of you get to bond, plus you get to lose any extra weight you might have gained. Getting some physical exercise will also help you to be agile, so that doing the house chores as you support your spouse during the pregnancy period will be easy.

Did you know? The longest pregnancy on record is that of Penny Diana Hunter in 1945. She was over 100 days due. The pregnancy lasted 12 months before the baby was born.

chapters.title: The best time to prepare for the challenging stages ahead is during the second trimester
chapters.text:
After the first trimester, it is now unlikely that your spouse will miscarry, so some congratulations will be in order. Now is the time for both of you to begin preparations for the arrival of the baby.

It's in the first trimester that your baby's body structure and organ systems develop.

Here is what to expect as you run through the second trimester:

Expect plenty of mood swings, forgetfulness, and some snoring as your spouse's hormone levels change. Also, because it is the second trimester, there is the opportunity for both of you to know the sex of your child. Both of you can jointly decide whether or not to know it. Labor pains are also frequent during the second trimester. Some will be real, others will be false. You and your spouse will need to work in tandem to differentiate between the real labor pains and the false ones.

Preparations for the final and stressful trimester also begin in the second trimester. This is the time to buy as much baby stuff as possible. If a baby shower is in the works, both of you should create a budget and work together towards it. Your spouse will likely want to start preparing the house, so it will be safe for the baby, therefore, you need to be ready to play your part.

Be proactive about the things you need to assemble, set up, or buy. Write them down in a jotter as soon as they cross your mind. Work through this list as soon as possible. Don't procrastinate. Are you intent on having a car seat installed for your baby? Do it now that the time is there, because a car seat is not something you want to install in a hurry. According to a 2008 sampling of 619 car seats in Pennsylvania, 80% were not correctly installed, and resulted in minor accidents.

Lastly, in the second trimester, both you and your spouse will have to start thinking of your child's names. It sounds like a fun task, but both of you will have to give it some careful thought. You want a name that's not silly, and that's not common.

chapters.title: Understand the childbirth process as your spouse's due date approaches
chapters.text:
By the end of the second trimester, you and your spouse would have gotten your home ready for your baby's arrival. You both would probably have agreed on a name for him or her, too. Your next task is to completely learn the birthing process.

You need to have a basic knowledge of what birthing a child involves. Birthing classes and videos would be of help here. Also, take time out to tour the hospital where your spouse will be giving birth. Intricately understand the layout of the hospital and the functional areas you will be frequenting. Where's the reception? The nursery? How do the reception and nursery link? It would be helpful if you can be up-to-speed with your knowledge of first aid. You never can tell when you might need the skill. You and your partner should also ponder on all possible delivery scenarios. Does she want a vaginal birth? Or a cesarean one? What happens to the umbilical cord blood and the placenta? Agree on what to do with these by–products of the birth process and make concrete arrangements based on your agreement.

While all of the preparations are ongoing, you and your spouse should take time out to enjoy your last days together as a couple without a child. Birthing a child is a life transforming experience, for both the man and woman, as your priorities will definitely change. It won't be a bad idea to take a final holiday with your spouse, prior to her giving birth. This is also the best time to arrange some time off from work, in the period just after the birth of your child, so you can be at home to tend to your spouse or child should any of them fall ill.

Ensure your bond is strong before bringing a child in, else it will be very difficult to keep the relationship alive.

How about your finances? Give it some planning. Understand what the costs are going to be like in the months ahead, and plan accordingly. Prioritize your expenses; do away with the luxury. Start saving up, for instance, for your kid's education.

You do a lot of growing up when you’re pregnant. It’s suddenly like, ‘Yikes. Here it is, folks. Playtime is over.’ ~ Connie Fioretto

chapters.title: In the final weeks leading to your spouse's due date, start baby watching and keep up the support level to your spouse
chapters.text:
Statistically, 98% of all women will give birth between weeks 38 and 42. Only 4% of all women birth their children on the due dates predicted to them.

Expectant couples have to be on a continual babywatch from week 38 as the baby can arrive anytime from then.

From the first day of the 9th month, get a hospital bag packed and placed somewhere easily accessible because your baby can come at any moment. You might have to leave with it in a hurry to a delivery room in your hospital. In the hospital bag, have good books to read, a camera, and contact information packed away. As much as it’s practicable, always be around your spouse. Anxiety about your pending parenthood is common. It's a panic attack you and your spouse can talk over and soothe. So long as the two of you are there for each other, to reassure each other, these panic attacks will pass.

There are health risks your spouse might have to contend with in these last weeks of the pregnancy, preterm labor being one. If your spouse suffers a preterm labor, her gynecologist would probably treat her and order her to begin bedrest for some time. Support her, be attentive to her. Keep her mind positively engaged.

Other details both of you will have to work out include whether or not to use painkillers during labor as well as to have the labor induced or to have a cesarean birth. Together, both of you should talk the options through with your spouse's gynecologist and arrive at a decision your spouse will be comfortable with.

Finally, if you feel despair just before or during the birth of your child, encourage yourself with the knowledge that your spouse is appreciative of all of the support you have extended her in the past nine months! Share your feelings, including your nervous moments, with her. Don't hide anything from her. Let her be there for you, just as you should be there for her. Both of you should live these moments to the fullest.

chapters.title: To succeed in your new roles as parents, keep communicating clearly, keep planning together and be supportive of each other
chapters.text:
Finally, your child has arrived. All required health checks have been done. It's time for your family to head home together. A new chapter in life has begun.

This is no time to rest on your oars. Rather, be ready for more decision-making. For example, how will your spouse agree to breastfeed the child? Will it be natural, which will be fully beneficial for both herself and the baby? Or will it be with the use of infant formula, a more practicable option but of less benefit to mother and child? Both of you should talk this through and arrive at a mutual decision.

According to recent studies, 15% of all new fathers suffer from postpartum depression. To prevent this from happening to you, create time and space for yourself to rejuvenate. Too often, people do not realize how stressful the pregnancy months have been for the male partner, and so tend to direct all of the love and attention to the mother and her child.

Engage your parents as both of you transition into parenthood. It's a good thing for a child to experience his or her parents’ care and love, and those of his or her grandparents, too. However, do set boundaries for the child's grandparents. Ultimately, you and your spouse are responsible for your child's upkeep, so make that clear to them. Welcome their advice, but seek out each other's counsel and act on them.

Once family and friends know that your spouse and the child have arrived home from the hospital, most will want to visit. Have clear policies in place for all such visits. Keep the visits short, but don't refuse their helping hands. You, your spouse and the child will need all the help you can get, plus your child's immune system is not strong yet.

Most gynecologists advise that couples abstain from sex for at least 6 weeks after childbirth, this is to enable physical recovery from delivery cuts.

Lastly, both you and your spouse will need to figure out how to deal with sleep deprivation. According to studies, parents miss out on the equivalent of six months of sleep in the first two years of their child's life. Make a plan for how both of you will handle this. Going forward, both of you should keep communicating, planning ahead and supporting each other, as you did in the last nine months.

Learn from yesterday, live for today, hope for tomorrow. The important thing is not to stop questioning. ~ Albert Einstein

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Pregnancy is one of the rare opportunities you have to show love. Try not to pass on it. So far in this summary, we've discussed various ways you can be a supportive and loving partner. However, it's important to recall that mindset is everything.

Your mental disposition towards helping your partner is more important than the tactics and strategies discussed here. It's important that you're willing and earnestly desiring to help her in every way you can. This disposition will enable you to notice little things you can do during her pregnancy and beyond — including things not discussed in this summary or taught to you by anyone.

Another thing you can do is never stop asking your partner how you can be of help. If you're reading this summary, chances are that this is the first time for you. Probably the first time for your partner, too. This means you two are clueless with no real world experience to draw insights from. So, you have to help each other. Aside from asking her directly, being an observant and present partner will enable you to notice things that need your attention.

After childbirth, you will initially find it difficult to adjust parenting with your normal life. This applies to both fathers and mothers even though it seems to be more in the mothers. No matter how much you learn from authors and coaches, you will still run into situations where you feel helpless and clueless. In cases like that, learn to trust your instincts. More importantly, don't let parenting choke the life out of you. It's good to devote your time and resources to raising children, but also remember that you have a life, too.

Try this

Follow your partner on her hospital visits. There are two reasons you should do this. First, it shows you're supportive. Second, it gets you familiar with the hospital environment. This way, you won't be wandering about on the day you take her to the hospital for delivery.

Read it till the end?
